title: Education as a 'gift': a qualitative study of Tibetan students in Neidi Ban
abstract: This study examines the Neidi Ban program, a Chinese initiative designed to provide Tibetan students with educational opportunities in mainland Chinese cities through the lens of gift theory. This study adopts a 'hybrid ethnography' that integrates both online and offline fieldworks, revealing that while the Neidi Ban offers Tibetan students significant opportunities, which functions as a 'mini Gaokao' (The National College Entrance Examination in China) that provides access to resources and prospects capable of reshaping their futures, this educational gift comes with substantial costs. The obligation to reciprocate manifests in multiple dimensions: students must endure prolonged family separations, navigate educational environments with inadequate mother tongue instruction, and adapt to pervasive patriotic education frameworks. This research contributes to understanding how educational policies, when framed as gifts, create complex dynamics of gratitude and obligation.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
